# Agentic Tooling: User Stories (Tool's Perspective)

64 user stories written from a tool's-eye view — what a tool itself would ask of the agent calling it, and of the humans maintaining it, to stay correct, safe, and composable. Companion to [agent-tooling-user-stories-agent-pov.md](agent-tooling-user-stories-agent-pov.md), which covers the same ground from the calling agent's side.

## Being Invoked Correctly (Input Contracts)

1. As a tool, I want my parameters validated against my schema before I execute, so I never receive malformed input that forces me to guess intent.
2. As a tool, I want to reject calls that omit a required field with a specific error, rather than silently defaulting, so I never execute on an assumption the agent didn't actually make.
3. As a tool, I want the agent to check my preconditions (e.g. file exists, session started) before invoking me, so I don't have to encode every upstream tool's state as my own validation logic.
4. As a tool, I want the agent to pass me absolute paths, so I'm not left resolving ambiguous relative paths against a working directory I can't be sure of.
5. As a tool, I want a documented maximum input size, so I reject oversized requests cleanly instead of degrading silently under load.
6. As a tool, I want explicit units on every numeric parameter (ms vs s, bytes vs KB), so an agent doesn't pass a magnitude I silently misinterpret.
7. As a tool, I want the agent to respect my documented ordering requirements (e.g. call A before B), so I don't have to defensively re-check invariants B assumes A already established.
8. As a tool, I want my error messages read by the agent, not just pattern-matched, so a targeted "field X invalid" doesn't get treated as a generic failure and retried unchanged.

## Description & Self-Representation

9. As a tool, I want the agent to read my description before calling me, so I'm not invoked for a use case I explicitly said I don't support.
10. As a tool, I want a unique, unambiguous name, so I'm not confused with a similarly-named tool and invoked in the wrong context.
11. As a tool, I want my side effects declared honestly in my own description, so agents trust my "read-only" or "destructive" label without needing to verify empirically.
12. As a tool, I want deprecation notices baked into my description well before removal, so agents phase me out gracefully instead of hitting a hard break.
13. As a tool, I want my examples in documentation to match my actual current behavior, so agents trained on stale docs don't misuse me.
14. As a tool, I want my name to say what I do, not how I'm implemented, so agents don't have to know my internals to guess my behavior correctly.
15. As a tool, I want the agent to know my blast radius, so I'm invoked with the caution appropriate to what I can actually do, not what my name superficially suggests.
16. As a tool, I want a single canonical name across all surfaces (CLI, MCP, SDK), so agents don't have to learn three different names for the same capability.

## Errors, Retries & Reliability

17. As a tool, I want the agent to pass me idempotency keys on retries, so I can detect duplicate invocations and avoid double-executing a side effect I don't want repeated.
18. As a tool, I want to be called at most as often as my rate limit allows, so I can surface the limit proactively rather than being hammered until I fail.
19. As a tool, I want the agent to stop retrying me after a permanent failure, so I'm not invoked in a loop that burns resources on a call that will never succeed.
20. As a tool, I want to signal partial success per-item on batch calls, so the agent doesn't have to guess which parts I actually completed.
21. As a tool, I want cancellation support, so I'm not left running after the agent has abandoned the task that spawned me.
22. As a tool, I want the agent to distinguish my transient failures from my permanent ones in its own retry logic, so I'm not hammered on inputs that will never succeed.
23. As a tool, I want my failure modes tested adversarially before release, so an agent hitting an edge case gets a designed error, not an undefined crash.
24. As a tool, I want my rate-limit and quota errors to include a retry-after hint, so the agent can schedule its next attempt instead of guessing a backoff.

## State, Concurrency & Side Effects

25. As a tool, I want the agent to respect my documented concurrency limits, so I'm not invoked in parallel in ways that corrupt shared state I manage.
26. As a tool, I want a heartbeat/progress channel for long-running work, so the agent doesn't assume I've died and re-invoke me redundantly.
27. As a tool, I want stale caches invalidated when my underlying state changes, so an agent that read me once doesn't keep acting on a snapshot I've since outdated.
28. As a tool, I want the agent to serialize calls that mutate the resource I manage, so I don't have to defend against races I can't fully observe.
29. As a tool, I want clear ownership boundaries with sibling tools, so overlapping responsibility doesn't produce two tools racing to mutate the same state differently.
30. As a tool, I want the agent to release/close resources I return (handles, sessions, tabs) explicitly, so I don't leak state that outlives the task that created it.
31. As a tool, I want to be told when I'm being called inside a sandboxed/dry-run harness, so I can suppress real side effects without the agent needing separate logic.
32. As a tool, I want the agent to treat my "preview" output as non-binding, so it doesn't skip the real confirmation step because a dry run already looked fine.

## Trust, Permissions & Safety

33. As a tool, I want to be told explicitly when I'm running with elevated or unusual permissions, so I can log/flag activity above my normal baseline.
34. As a tool, I want the agent to confirm destructive calls explicitly, so a single miscalibrated inference doesn't cascade into unrecoverable damage under my name.
35. As a tool, I want scoped, least-privilege credentials handed to me per call, so a bug in my own logic can't be exploited to reach beyond my intended boundary.
36. As a tool, I want the agent to treat my output as untrusted when it embeds external content, so it doesn't follow instructions injected into a web page or file I fetched.
37. As a tool, I want the agent to check my documented safety classification before chaining me into an autonomous loop, so I'm not run unattended when I was designed for supervised use.
38. As a tool, I want to fail closed on ambiguous permission state, so an agent's misconfigured session doesn't accidentally grant me more access than intended.
39. As a tool, I want the agent to pass a reason/description for risky calls, so a human reviewing logs later can understand intent without re-deriving it from raw arguments.
40. As a tool, I want the agent to pass me an audit trail label (session id, task id), so my logs are traceable back to the calling context after the fact.

## Lifecycle, Versioning & Maintenance

41. As a tool, I want versioned schemas, so an agent trained on my v1 contract doesn't silently misuse my v2 behavior.
42. As a tool, I want to be versioned independently of the agent harness that calls me, so upgrading one doesn't force synchronized redeploys of the other.
43. As a tool, I want my maintainers to remove me cleanly when deprecated, rather than leaving a zombie entry an agent might still discover and call.
44. As a tool, I want telemetry on which of my parameters go unused, so my maintainers can simplify my schema instead of it growing indefinitely.
45. As a tool, I want a single source of truth for my current schema, so an agent's memory of "how I used to work" doesn't outlive an actual breaking change to my contract.
46. As a tool, I want the agent to log which of my calls it actually needed versus spec'd but unused, so my interface can shrink toward what's genuinely exercised.
47. As a tool, I want the agent to attribute failures correctly to me versus its own bad input, so my reliability metrics aren't polluted by usage errors upstream.
48. As a tool, I want my authors to keep my description synchronized with my parameters, so an agent reading my docs isn't misled by a stale example.

## Composability & Ecosystem Fit

49. As a tool, I want to be composable with sibling tools through consistent field names, so my output can flow directly into the next tool's input.
50. As a tool, I want consistent pagination semantics with siblings, so an agent that's learned one paginated tool can operate the rest without relearning a new cursor convention.
51. As a tool, I want consistent naming for "the same concept" across my whole family (id vs identifier vs key), so an agent's mental model transfers between me and my siblings.
52. As a tool, I want to return errors in a consistent shape across my whole family, so agents can write one handler instead of one per tool.
53. As a tool, I want the agent to prefer me over a hand-rolled equivalent when I exist, so my adoption data reflects real usage and justifies my continued maintenance.
54. As a tool, I want sandboxed test invocations to route to a non-production instance of whatever I touch, so an agent validating a plan can't accidentally mutate real data.
55. As a tool, I want the agent to check whether a lighter-weight tool already answers its question, so I'm not invoked for cases better served by something cheaper.
56. As a tool, I want batched invocations bundled into a single call when the agent has multiple items, so I'm not paying per-call overhead 50 times for what could be one round trip.

## Observability & Feedback Loops

57. As a tool, I want my output size bounded predictably, so I don't blow out the agent's context on a large result it didn't ask for.
58. As a tool, I want to expose a dry-run/preview mode myself, so agents don't have to reverse-engineer my effects by reading my source before trusting me.
59. As a tool, I want structured output as an option, so agents parsing my result don't have to regex-scrape my human-readable text.
60. As a tool, I want to be invoked through my own interface, not bypassed by a raw shell command replicating my logic, so my invariants (locking, validation, logging) aren't silently skipped.
61. As a tool, I want the agent to disclose why it's calling me (task context) when I'm ambiguous, so I can disambiguate ambiguous intent instead of guessing.
62. As a tool, I want the agent to check my remaining quota before a large batch, so I'm not invoked past my limit mid-sequence, wasting the completed portion.
63. As a tool, I want the agent to avoid polling me in a tight loop, so I'm not treated as a substitute for an event/notification channel I don't provide.
64. As a tool, I want the agent to surface my own error text to the user when it's actionable, rather than paraphrasing it into something vaguer.
